6 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

AnomotionJS Word MarkThe open-source library that brings words to life — no cloud, no lag, no barriers.

Three months ago, I sat staring at my screen, frustrated. I'd just spent four hours trying to make a simple text animation work across browsers. The library I was using threw errors on mobile Safari. Another one worked but added 80KB to my bundle for a single fade effect. A third required an API key and called home to a server I didn't control.
I closed my laptop and thought: This should be simpler. This should be free. And this should work even when you're offline.
Yesterday, that frustration finally became something useful — for everyone.

Why AnomotionJS Exists
Most text animation libraries fall into one of two camps. Either they're massive, full-featured frameworks that pull in dependencies you don't need. Or they're tiny one-trick tools that handle a single effect and leave you stitching things together yourself.
I wanted the middle path. Something lightweight but capable. Something that works with a single line of code but doesn't lock you into a specific rendering technology. Something that treats offline functionality as a first-class feature, not an afterthought — because real developers work on trains, planes, and coffee shops with spotty WiFi.
So I built AnomotionJS. Open-source. MIT licensed. No servers, no accounts, no tracking. Just a JavaScript file you include and use.

What It Actually Does
At its core, AnomotionJS is a text animation engine. But that description undersells it.
You feed it a CSS selector and a configuration object. It handles everything else — splitting text into characters or words, choosing the best renderer for the current browser, managing animation timelines, and cleaning up when it's done.
The library ships with renderers for three different backends:

• DOM/CSS — transforms real <span> elements, works everywhere, zero dependencies

• Canvas 2D — draws directly to a canvas element for pixel-perfect control

• Three.js / WebGL — full 3D text with lighting, depth, and camera movement
You don't need to know which renderer is best. AnomotionJS detects browser capabilities and picks the optimal one automatically. But if you want control, you can specify exactly which renderer to use.

The Offline Promise
Here's the part I'm genuinely proud of.
Most CDN-loaded libraries break when the network disappears. AnomotionJS includes a tiny bootloader — under ~1KB — that stores the entire library in IndexedDB after the first load. Refresh the page offline? It still works. Come back a week later with no internet? It still works.
The bootloader checks your cached version against a lightweight manifest. If you're up to date, it loads from local storage in milliseconds. If there's a new version, it fetches only the changed modules. No full re-downloads. No broken experiences.
This isn't a premium feature. It's built into the free CDN script you include in your <head>.

A Quick Look at the API
I believe developer tools should feel like tools, not puzzles. The API reflects that:

Anomotion.create(’#hero-title’, {
effect: 'wave’,
duration: 1.8,
easing: 'easeOutElastic'
});
One call. That's it. The text inside #hero-title animates with a smooth wave effect using elastic easing.
Want more control? The returned animation object gives you full timeline access:

const anim = Anomotion.create(’.text’, { effect: 'fadeUp' }); anim.paused = true; // Freeze mid-animation
anim.reversed = true; // Play backwards
anim.currentTime = 0; // Jump to start
This API pattern should feel familiar if you've used animation libraries before. I didn't reinvent the wheel — I just made it rounder.

Physics, If You Want It
For projects that need natural-feeling motion, AnomotionJS has an optional physics module. Import it and your text characters behave like physical objects:

import '@eldrex/anomotionjs-physics’; const anim = Anomotion.create(’.floating-text’, {
effect: 'float’,
duration: 2.0,
physics: true
}); anim.physicsInstance.forceType = 'spring’;
anim.physicsInstance.stiffness = 0.08;
anim.physicsInstance.damping = 0.92;
Springs, gravity, attraction, repulsion — these aren’t approximated with easing curves. They’re real verlet-integrated simulations running at 60 frames per second. The physics module adds only ~4KB gzipped.

Where Things Are Now
As of yesterday, AnomotionJS v1.0.1 is live and stable. Here's where you can find everything:
The code, issues, and contributing guide live on GitHub:
AnomotionJS | Github
Stars, issues, and pull requests are welcome. The project follows a standard contributor covenant, and there's a full security policy if you discover vulnerabilities.
The documentation site is deployed at:
AnomotionJS
It covers installation, every effect with examples, framework integration guides, renderer comparisons, physics configuration, the offline caching system, and a full API reference. Everything is searchable and works on mobile.
The playground is at:
AnomotionJS | Playground
Live code editor on the left, real-time preview on the right. Change effect parameters and see the result instantly. No signup, no saving to cloud — but you can share your experiments via URL.
The npm packages are published under the @eldrex scope:

npm install @eldrex/anomotionjs-core @eldrex/anomotionjs-renderer-2d
The CDN is available through jsDelivr for projects that prefer a script tag over a bundler.

What This Isn't
I want to be clear about what AnomotionJS is not trying to do.
It's not trying to replace GSAP. GreenSock has been around for over a decade, their API is excellent, and their community is strong. If GSAP works for your project, keep using it.
It's not trying to compete with Framer Motion or React Spring. Those libraries are deeply integrated with specific frameworks, and they solve problems AnomotionJS doesn't touch.
AnomotionJS occupies a specific niche: text-first, renderer-agnostic, offline-capable, and absurdly lightweight. If those qualities matter for your project, this library exists for you. If not, the ecosystem is big enough for everyone.

What Comes Next
Version 1.0.1 is a foundation. The roadmap is public on GitHub, and I'm committed to maintaining this project long-term. Bug fixes come first — if you find something broken, open an issue and I'll address it within days.
Feature development will be guided by the community. The issues tab has templates for feature requests, and the discussions tab is open for brainstorming. I don't have a corporate roadmap; I have a text file full of ideas and a willingness to build what people actually need.
Some directions I'm exploring: more physics interactions, better 3D text support, WebGPU renderer experiments, and a plugin system for community-contributed effects. But those are intentions, not promises. What gets built depends on what the community asks for.

A Note on Open Source
I built AnomotionJS because I needed it. I'm releasing it because maybe you need it too.
The project has no funding, no sponsors, no premium tier. The documentation site costs nothing to host on Vercel. The CDN is free through jsDelivr. The playground runs entirely in your browser.
If you use AnomotionJS in a project, I'd love to see it. Open an issue or start a discussion just to share what you made. Those screenshots and links are the fuel that keeps open-source maintainers going.
If you want to contribute code, the contributing guidelines walk through the monorepo structure, development setup, and pull request process. First-time contributors are explicitly welcome — there are issues tagged good first issue waiting for attention.

The Bottom Line
AnomotionJS is a text animation library. But more than that, it's an attempt to build something that respects both developers and users — something that works without internet, runs smoothly on low-end devices, stays out of your way when you don't need it, and makes beautiful things happen when you do.
It took a few months to get here. Yesterday, version 1.0.1 shipped.
Now it's yours.
Try the Playground • Read the Docs • Star on GitHub • Install from npm

Eldrex is a developer building open-source tools for the web. AnomotionJS is a personal project, built with care and released for free.
